Dissection with the harmonic scalpel is then undertaken to mobilize the tumor away from the mediastinum.
In this patient, the right upper lobe tumor was invasive to the mediastinal pleura, but not involving mediastinal lymph nodes as defined by preoperative mediastinoscopy.
Under direct vision, it is clear that invasion of the mediastinal pleura is evident, yet invasion of mediastinal structures, such as the superior pulmonary vein or trachea, are excluded.
After completion of the mediastinal dissection, the distal aspect of the asagus vein, at its confluence with the superior vena cava, is stapled and divided.

In this video, right middle lobectomy is undertaken.

and access for the stapler across the hilum is improved by initial division of the major fissure.
For middle lobectomy, the stapler can be passed from the anterior port as demonstrated here, or it can be passed parallel to the hilum from the camera port as is done for an upper lobectomy.
After division of the pulmonary vein, the middle lobe bronchus is easily dissected using a curved dissecting clamp.
